title: Indian subcontinent

infobox.value: [External](/wiki/Dependent_territory "Dependent territory") (1)   * [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory")[[note 5]](#cite_note-BIOT-5) (United Kingdom)  [Disputed](/wiki/List_of_territorial_disputes "List of territorial disputes") (1)   * [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir")[[note 6]](#cite_note-Kashmir-6)

Official:   * [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") * [Dzongkha](/wiki/Dzongkha "Dzongkha") * [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") * [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") * [Maldivian](/wiki/Maldivian_language "Maldivian language") * [Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language") * [Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language") * [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") * [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu")

*List:*   * [Bangladesh Standard Time](/wiki/Bangladesh_Standard_Time "Bangladesh Standard Time") (BST) * [Bhutan Time](/wiki/Bhutan_Time "Bhutan Time") (BTT) * [Indian Standard Time](/wiki/Indian_Standard_Time "Indian Standard Time") (IST) * [Maldives Time](/wiki/Time_in_Maldives "Time in Maldives") (MVT) * [Nepal Standard Time](/wiki/Nepal_Standard_Time "Nepal Standard Time") (NPT) * [Pakistan Standard Time](/wiki/Pakistan_Standard_Time "Pakistan Standard Time") (PST) * [Sri Lanka Standard Time](/wiki/Sri_Lanka_Standard_Time "Sri Lanka Standard Time") (SLST)

is a [physiographic region](/wiki/Physiographic_region "Physiographic region") of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") below the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") which projects into the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") between the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal") to the east and the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") to the west. It is now divided between [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").[[1]](#cite_note-oed-indian-subcontinent1-7) Although the terms "Indian subcontinent" and "South Asia" are often also used interchangeably to denote a wider region which includes, in addition, [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), the "Indian subcontinent" is more of a [geophysical](/wiki/Geophysical "Geophysical") term, whereas "South Asia" is more [geopolitical](/wiki/Geopolitical "Geopolitical").[[2]](#cite_note-oed-indian-subcontinent2-8) "South Asia" frequently also includes [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), which is not considered part of the subcontinent even in extended usage.[[3]](#cite_note-norwineplus-9)

in many historical sources. Even today, historians use this term to denote the entire Indian subcontinent when discussing history up until the era of the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). Over time, however, "India" evolved to refer to a distinct [political entity](/wiki/Political_entity "Political entity") that eventually became a nation-state ([today the Republic of India](/wiki/History_of_the_Republic_of_India "History of the Republic of India")).[[4]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica_2022_t419-10)

Physiographical region in South Asia

This article is about the physiographical region of Asia. For the geographical subregion of Asia, see [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). "The subcontinent" redirects here. For general usage of the term, see [Continent § Subcontinents](/wiki/Continent#Subcontinents "Continent").

The **Indian subcontinent** is a [physiographic region](/wiki/Physiographic_region "Physiographic region") of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") below the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") which projects into the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") between the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal") to the east and the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") to the west. It is now divided between [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").[[1]](#cite_note-oed-indian-subcontinent1-7) Although the terms "Indian subcontinent" and "South Asia" are often also used interchangeably to denote a wider region which includes, in addition, [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), the "Indian subcontinent" is more of a [geophysical](/wiki/Geophysical "Geophysical") term, whereas "South Asia" is more [geopolitical](/wiki/Geopolitical "Geopolitical").[[2]](#cite_note-oed-indian-subcontinent2-8) "South Asia" frequently also includes [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), which is not considered part of the subcontinent even in extended usage.[[3]](#cite_note-norwineplus-9)

## Name

Historically, the region surrounding and southeast of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") was often simply referred to as **India** in many historical sources. Even today, historians use this term to denote the entire Indian subcontinent when discussing history up until the era of the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). Over time, however, "India" evolved to refer to a distinct [political entity](/wiki/Political_entity "Political entity") that eventually became a nation-state ([today the Republic of India](/wiki/History_of_the_Republic_of_India "History of the Republic of India")).[[4]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica_2022_t419-10)

According to the [Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary"), the term *[subcontinent](/wiki/Continent#Subcontinents "Continent")* signifies a "subdivision of a continent which has a distinct geographical, political, or cultural identity" and also a "large land mass somewhat smaller than a continent".[[5]](#cite_note-11)[[6]](#cite_note-OED-12) Its use to signify the Indian subcontinent is evidenced from the early twentieth century when most of the territory was either part of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") or allied with them.[[7]](#cite_note-13)[[8]](#cite_note-14) It was a convenient term to refer to the region comprising both [British India](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India") and the [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state").[[9]](#cite_note-15)[[10]](#cite_note-16)

The term has been particularly common in the British Empire and its successors,[[11]](#cite_note-17) while the term *South Asia* is the more common usage in Europe and North America as well as in most countries in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") itself sometimes.[[12]](#cite_note-18)[[13]](#cite_note-19) According to historians [Sugata Bose](/wiki/Sugata_Bose "Sugata Bose") and [Ayesha Jalal](/wiki/Ayesha_Jalal "Ayesha Jalal"), the Indian subcontinent has come to be known as South Asia "in more recent and neutral parlance".[[14]](#cite_note-sugata-20) [Indologist](/wiki/Indology "Indology") Ronald B. Inden argues that the usage of the term *South Asia* is becoming more widespread since it clearly distinguishes the region from East Asia.[[15]](#cite_note-inden-21) While *South Asia*, a more accurate term that reflects the region's contemporary political demarcations, is replacing the *Indian subcontinent*, a term closely linked to the region's colonial heritage, as a cover term, the latter is still widely used in typological studies.[[16]](#cite_note-22)[[17]](#cite_note-23)

Since the [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"), citizens of Pakistan (which became independent of British India in 1947) and Bangladesh (which became independent of Pakistan in 1971) often perceive the use of the *Indian subcontinent* as offensive and suspicious because of the dominant placement of India in the term.[[18]](#cite_note-farmerplus-24) As such it is being increasingly less used in those countries.[[note 7]](#cite_note-Indo-Pakistan-26) Meanwhile, many Indian analysts prefer to use the term because of the socio-cultural commonalities of the region.[[18]](#cite_note-farmerplus-24) The region has also been called the "Asian subcontinent",[[20]](#cite_note-27)[[21]](#cite_note-28) the "South Asian subcontinent",[[22]](#cite_note-29)[[23]](#cite_note-30)[[24]](#cite_note-31)[[25]](#cite_note-32) as well as "India" or "[Greater India](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India")" in the classical and pre-modern sense.[[26]](#cite_note-McLeod_p1-33)[[3]](#cite_note-norwineplus-9)[[27]](#cite_note-mittal-34)[[28]](#cite_note-Kathleen_M_page_10-35)

The sport of [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), introduced to the region by the British, is [notably popular](/wiki/Cricket_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Cricket in the Indian subcontinent") in [India](/wiki/Cricket_in_India "Cricket in India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Cricket_in_Pakistan "Cricket in Pakistan"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Cricket_in_Sri_Lanka "Cricket in Sri Lanka"), [Nepal](/wiki/Cricket_in_Nepal "Cricket in Nepal"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Cricket_in_Bangladesh "Cricket in Bangladesh") and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Cricket_in_Afghanistan "Cricket in Afghanistan"). Within a cricket context, these countries are sometimes referred to simply as *the subcontinent* e.g. "[Australia's](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team") tour of the subcontinent".[[29]](#cite_note-36) The term is also sometimes used adjectivally in cricket e.g. "subcontinental conditions".[[30]](#cite_note-37)[[31]](#cite_note-38)

## Geology

See also: [Indian plate](/wiki/Indian_plate "Indian plate") and [Himalayas § Geology](/wiki/Himalayas#Geology "Himalayas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/230_Ma_plate_tectonic_reconstruction.png/250px-230_Ma_plate_tectonic_reconstruction.png)](/wiki/File:230_Ma_plate_tectonic_reconstruction.png)

[Cimmeria](/wiki/Cimmeria_(continent) "Cimmeria (continent)"), having rifted from Gondwana shown drifting towards Eurasia, closing the Paleo-Tethys Ocean above, opening the Neo-Tethys Ocean below, and carrying parts of what is today the [Tibetan Plateau](/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau "Tibetan Plateau")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Karakoram_Kohistan_Ladakh_Gangdese_Belt.jpg/250px-Karakoram_Kohistan_Ladakh_Gangdese_Belt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karakoram_Kohistan_Ladakh_Gangdese_Belt.jpg)

The accretions of the [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram"), the Kohistan-Ladakh [island arc](/wiki/Island_arc "Island arc"), and the [Gangdese belt](/wiki/Gangdese_batholith "Gangdese batholith") to Eurasia preceded the final India-Eurasia collision. The stars mark the [syntaxis](/wiki/Syntaxis_(geology) "Syntaxis (geology)")-causing obtrustions.

Before the [Indian plate](/wiki/Indian_plate "Indian plate") rifted from [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") and drifted northward toward Eurasia, two other landmasses, the [Qiangtang terrane](/wiki/Qiangtang_terrane "Qiangtang terrane") and [Lhasa terrane](/wiki/Lhasa_terrane "Lhasa terrane"),[[note 8]](#cite_note-40) had accreted to Eurasia.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrischMeschedeBlakey2011174-41) The Qiantang and Lhasa terranes were part of the string of microcontinents [Cimmeria](/wiki/Cimmeria_(continent) "Cimmeria (continent)"), today constituting parts of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") (including the [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram")[[34]](#cite_note-karakoram-cimmerian-42)), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), which closed the [Paleo-Tethys Ocean](/wiki/Paleo-Tethys_Ocean "Paleo-Tethys Ocean") above them and opening the Neo-Tethys Ocean between them and Gondwana, eventually colliding with Eurasia, and creating the [Cimmerian Orogeny](/wiki/Cimmerian_Orogeny "Cimmerian Orogeny").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrischMeschedeBlakey2011172-43)

After the Lhasa terrane had adjoined Eurasia, an active continental margin opened along its southern flank, below which the Neo-Tethys oceanic plate had begun to subduct. [Magmatic activity](/wiki/Magmatic_activity "Magmatic activity") along this flank produced the [Gangdese batholith](/wiki/Gangdese_batholith "Gangdese batholith") in what is today the [Tibetan trans-Himalaya](/wiki/Transhimalaya "Transhimalaya"). Another subduction zone opened to the west, in the ocean basin above the Kohistan-Ladakh [island arc](/wiki/Island_arc "Island arc"). This island arc—formed by one oceanic plate subducting beneath another, its magma rising and creating continental crust—drifted north, closed its ocean basin and collided with Eurasia.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrischMeschedeBlakey2011173-44) [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh") is today in the Indian-administered region of [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") and [Kohistan](/wiki/Kohistan,_Pakistan "Kohistan, Pakistan") in the [Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa") province of Pakistan, both on the Indian subcontinent.

The collision of India with Eurasia closed the Neo-Tethys Ocean.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrischMeschedeBlakey2011172-43) The suture zone (in this instance, the remnants of the Neo-Tethys subduction zone pinched between the two continental crusts), which marks India's welding to Eurasia, is called the [Indus-Yarlung suture zone](/wiki/Indus-Yarlung_suture_zone "Indus-Yarlung suture zone").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrischMeschedeBlakey2011172-43) It lies north of the Himalayas. The headwaters of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") and the [Yarlung Tsangpo](/wiki/Yarlung_Tsangpo "Yarlung Tsangpo") (later in its course, the [Brahmaputra](/wiki/Brahmaputra "Brahmaputra")) flow along this suture zone.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrischMeschedeBlakey2011172-43) These two Eurasian rivers, whose courses were continually diverted by the rising Himalayas, define the western and eastern limits, respectively, of the Himalayan mountain range.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrischMeschedeBlakey2011172-43)
